literal: anger, wrath, rage; action-noun from √krudh 'to be angry, to be wrathful' (krudh + ghañ → krodha)
invariant_definition: *Krodha* is anger or wrath: the aversive, heated reaction that arises when a desire is thwarted or an object of attachment is threatened. Across the Indian traditions it is generally classed among the inner enemies (*ari-ṣaḍ-varga*: *kāma, krodha, lobha, moha, mada, mātsarya*) or the afflictions of the agent rather than as a neutral emotion. Its characteristic doctrinal placement is causal: anger is treated as a consequence of obstructed desire and an antecedent of further ruin. The *Bhagavad-Gītā* gives the standard statement of this sequence — from dwelling on sense-objects comes attachment (*saṅga*), from attachment desire (*kāma*), from obstructed desire anger (*krodha*); from anger delusion (*sammoha*), from delusion loss of memory, from loss of memory the destruction of the discriminating intellect (*buddhi-nāśa*), and from that the ruin of the person (2.62–63). Anger is also named a 'gate of hell' alongside *kāma* and *lobha* (16.21). In *Dharmaśāstra* and epic ethical literature *akrodha* (freedom from anger) and *kṣamā* (forbearance) are listed among the duties of the disciplined and of the brāhmaṇa. In Pātañjala Yoga the corresponding standing affliction is *dveṣa*, the repulsion paired with *rāga*. *Krodha* is thus typically analyzed not as an isolated feeling but as a link in a causal chain — taken as evidence of residual craving and as a cause of the loss of judgment.

school_framing.shared_core: *Krodha* names **the aversive heat that arises when desire is obstructed or an attachment is threatened**; the traditions converge in classing it among the inner enemies or afflictions and in treating its mastery (*akrodha*, *kṣamā*) as a mark of the disciplined self. What varies is the analytic vocabulary — a link in a bondage-chain, a member of the *ṣaḍ-varga*, or the *dveṣa* affliction of Yoga — not the phenomenon.
school_framing.register_axes_note: Largely **shared**: *krodha* is a broadly agreed-upon vice rather than a contested technical category. The chief difference of framing is between the ethical-soteriological treatment (anger as a link from thwarted desire to the collapse of *buddhi*) and the yogic treatment (the underlying repulsion as the standing *kleśa* *dveṣa*); both diagnose the same aversive movement.

per_school.definition: In the *Gītā* *krodha* is a link in the descent into bondage: born of frustrated *kāma*, it issues in delusion and the destruction of discriminative judgment (2.62–63), and is named a gate of hell (16.21). Śaṅkara and later commentators read freedom from anger as the practical sign that desire itself has been mastered, since anger cannot arise where there is no thwarted craving; anger is thus treated diagnostically — as evidence of residual attachment — rather than as an isolated emotion to be merely suppressed.
primary_loci: Bhagavad-Gītā 3.37 (krodha with kāma as the enemy)
Bhagavad-Gītā 16.21 (krodha as a gate of hell)

per_school.definition: In Pātañjala Yoga the aversive affliction is *dveṣa*, aversion that follows upon pain (*duḥkhānuśayī dveṣaḥ*, YS 2.8), one of the five *kleśas* rooted in *avidyā*. *Krodha* is the manifest, agitated form of this standing repulsion disturbing the *citta*. As a *kleśa*-product it is to be attenuated by *kriyā-yoga* and dissolved as discrimination matures, since while it operates the mind cannot reach steady absorption.

Anger appears as *dosa/dveṣa* (hatred, ill-will), one of the three unwholesome roots (*akusala-mūla*: *lobha, dosa, moha*) and one of the three fires whose extinction is *nibbāna*. Ill-will (*byāpāda*) is the second of the five hindrances to concentration. Its antidote is the cultivation of loving-kindness (*mettā*) and patience (*khanti*). As with the Brāhmaṇical accounts, anger is a defilement to be uprooted rather than a passing mood, but it is here a root-poison rather than a link from a prior desire.
primary_loci: the three akusala-mūla and three fires (lobha, dosa, moha) in the suttas
the five nīvaraṇa (byāpāda); mettā as antidote

textual_history.summary: The root √krudh ('to be angry') is old, but *krodha* as a named inner enemy is not a developed theme of the earliest Vedic layer; the Ṛgveda speaks far more of external foes and the gods' wrath than of anger as a fault of the self. The term comes into focus in the late-Vedic and Upaniṣadic period — the Bṛhadāraṇyaka can already list *krodhamaya* ('made of anger') among the self's modes alongside *kāmamaya* — and is fully crystallised in the *Bhagavad-Gītā*, which gives anger its classic diagnostic placement: born of obstructed desire, issuing in delusion and the collapse of judgment (2.62–63), and named with *kāma* and *lobha* as a 'gate of hell' (16.21). The Buddhist three-poison scheme (greed–hatred–delusion, with *dveṣa/dosa* the hatred-pole) runs parallel and is roughly contemporary.

stages.sanskrit: muhyantv anye abhito janāsa ihāsmākam maghavā sūrir astu
stages.rendering: Let the other folk round about be bewildered; here let our generous patron be [our] inspired seer.
stages.note: Cited not for *krodha* — the noun is not a thematic Vedic term — but to register the Ṛgvedic baseline of the affect cluster: the verbs of the family (here √muh, 'be confused') are present, while the later analytic nouns (krodha, moha, rāga as faults of the self) are not yet doctrinal categories. The earliest layer treats wrath chiefly as the gods' or the enemy's, not as an inner enemy to be conquered.

stages.rendering: [This self is] made of desire and not made of desire, made of anger and not made of anger, made of dharma and not made of dharma — made of all.
stages.note: The earliest clean naming of *krodha* (and *kāma*) in our corpus: Yājñavalkya's teaching that the self takes on the character it acts from — 'as one acts, as one conducts oneself, so one becomes' (yathākārī yathācārī tathā bhavati, same verse). Anger is here a mode the self assumes, not yet the link in a causal descent it becomes in the Gītā.

stages.sanskrit: saṅgāt saṃjāyate kāmaḥ kāmāt krodho 'bhijāyate | krodhād bhavati saṃmohaḥ saṃmohāt smṛti-vibhramaḥ | smṛti-bhraṃśād buddhi-nāśo buddhi-nāśāt praṇaśyati
stages.rendering: From attachment is born desire; from desire anger arises. From anger comes thorough delusion; from delusion the wandering of memory; from the lapse of memory the destruction of the discriminating intellect; and from that destruction one is lost.
stages.note: The Gītā's signature placement of *krodha*: not an isolated mood but the link from thwarted *kāma* to the collapse of *buddhi*. Śaṅkara glosses *saṃmoha* here as *aviveka*, the loss of the discrimination of what is and is not to be done — anger is treated diagnostically, as evidence of residual craving. Cf. 3.37, where *kāma* and *krodha* together are 'the enemy here' (viddhy enam iha vairiṇam).

stages.sanskrit: tri-vidhaṃ narakasyedaṃ dvāraṃ nāśanam ātmanaḥ | kāmaḥ krodhas tathā lobhas tasmād etat trayaṃ tyajet
stages.rendering: This is the threefold gate of hell, destructive of the self — desire, anger, and greed; therefore let one abandon these three.
stages.note: The 'gates of hell' verse that fixes *krodha* in a fixed triad with *kāma* and *lobha*, the seed of the later *ari-ṣaḍ-varga* ('six inner enemies'). Note the structural parallel to the Buddhist three unwholesome roots (*lobha–dveṣa–moha*): the same three affects recur, catalogued differently.

translator_note: 'Anger' is correct, but in this literature *krodha* is not a momentary mood. It is analyzed as a link between thwarted desire and the loss of judgment, a member of the six inner enemies, or the manifest form of the *dveṣa* affliction, so its occurrence is treated as a symptom of attachment rather than an independent feeling. Renderings such as 'irritation' or 'frustration' understate it: the texts treat *krodha* as a cause of *buddhi-nāśa* (the destruction of the discriminating intellect), and its opposite *akrodha* is listed as a virtue in its own right.
